title: From The Whole To A Part
study: A Study In The Life Of John The
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN THE GOSPELS Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to highlight the ministry of John the Baptist and to frame up the gap between the Old and New Testament timelines.
weekTheme: Boys overlook, Men seek To find your place in God's story you must understand God's story. THE STORY IS BIGGER, WIDER, AND LONGER THAN YOU THINK Most people get bored with the Bible because they don’t understand the big storyline of it. And because they miss that, they fail to see how their own life fits into what God is doing in the world. That can mean the bible is seen as an old, boring book, disconnected from anything to do with our lives and struggles. But knowing what is happening in the story can wake up a dead man’s heart, capture a bored man’s attention, and invite him into a story of adventure, risk, courage, and reward, bigger than he ever thought possible. This week, you are going to see the background and context surrounding how John the Baptist was born. He was a radical man who appeared to come out of nowhere, shook the whole nation, and then had his head cut off by a tyrant king because a young woman did an erotic dance in front of him. It’s a story of passion, power, courage, and love, and one that has real implications for how you view yourself as a man. But knowing the events surrounding his life helps you understand what happened in his life. So let’s jump in and fill in some gaps in your understanding of the biblical story, so you can take your place in God's larger story. Let's go.

days.title: Worth The Wait
days.scripture: PSALM 40:1-3 I waited patiently for the LORD to help me, and he turned to me and heard my cry. He lifted me out of the pit of despair, out of the mud and the mire. He set my feet on solid ground and steadied me as I walked along. He has given me a new song to sing, a hymn of praise to our God. Many will see what he has done and be amazed. They will put their trust in the LORD.
days.reflection: What’s the longest you have had to wait for something that you really wanted? A week? A year? Maybe you have wanted something since you were a little kid, and you still haven’t got it. When you wait a long time for something and you finally get it, you get super excited. When you realize how long people had to wait for things they really wanted in the Bible, you will understand why they got so excited when they finally appear. It helps us make sense of why people freaked out when John the Baptist showed up. Here are the last verses in the Old Testament: Malachi 4:5-6 “Look, I am sending you the prophet Elijah before the great and dreadful day of the LORD arrives. His preaching will turn the hearts of fathers to their children, and the hearts of children to their fathers. Otherwise, I will come and strike the land with a curse.” Now here are the first words of the New Testament. Matthew 1:1 This is a record of the ancestors of Jesus the Messiah, a descendant of David and of Abraham. What is the connection there? It feels kind of hard to make the last words of the Old Testament connect with the first words of the New Testament. You know why? Because there is a 400-year gap between them. 400 years! The thing to understand is this: even though these are sometimes called “the silent years” by bible scholars, God was still working. Like a master game designer, he was working to position his people into the right time and place so they would be open and ready to receive his message about Jesus as the Messiah. As you grow into manhood, you will have to learn that silence does not equal absence. Just because God doesn’t seem to be saying a lot, doesn’t mean that he isn’t doing a lot. God is working while we are waiting. God is preparing while we are praying. God is creating while we are crying out. He did this between the Old and New Testament, and he will do this in your life. You were not born at this time of history for nothing. God has placed you here. Of all the moments you could have been born, this is the one most ripe for you to fulfill your calling. This is the moment for you to serve God. This is the moment for your life to matter. Open your eyes and begin to pay attention to what God may be preparing you for. Your greatest point of frustration now may bear the greatest fruit in the days to come. Don’t lose heart; God is doing more than you know.
questions: You are a part of a generation called “Gen Z.” What are some of the ways your generation views life differently than your parents’ or grandparents’ generation?

Over the last few shifts in the Primal Path, we have talked a lot about the importance of patience. It’s an important part of the character of a man. Do you see yourself growing in patience? Can you give a couple of examples?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they have seen God prepare them to serve him in this time of history. In what ways can they recognize God's timing and planning for them?

thoughts.verse: The Lord isn’t really being slow about his promise, as some people think. No, he is being patient for your sake. He does not want anyone to be destroyed, but wants everyone to repent.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and ask God to show you where he is working around you. Ask him to open your eyes to things that he has been working on that are coming to light now.

days.title: Once Upon A _______?
days.scripture: JOHN 1:1-9 In the beginning the Word already existed. The Word was with God, and the Word was God. He existed in the beginning with God. God created everything through him, and nothing was created except through him. The Word gave life to everything that was created, and his life brought light to everyone. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness can never extinguish it. God sent a man, John the Baptist, to tell about the light so that everyone might believe because of his testimony. John himself was not the light; he was simply a witness to tell about the light. The one who is the true light, who gives light to everyone, was coming into the world.
days.reflection: What is the first thing that comes to mind when you hear these words? A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away… In a hole in the ground, there lived a hobbit… Once upon a time… The beginning of a story is incredibly important because it helps you understand what will happen in the story. Have you ever missed the first 10 minutes of a movie, and then tried to figure out what is going on? It can be confusing and frustrating to know who each character is and what the plot is. The same thing can happen when reading the Bible if you don’t understand the context. In this series, we are looking at the life of John the Baptist. He played an important role in the story of Jesus and has a lot to teach us about what it means to be a man. When he burst on the scene, he was incredibly popular and got the nation in an uproar. But why were the people so ready for a man like him? Understanding the context is key. The expectation for John’s ministry didn’t start when he preached. It started in the period between the Old and New Testaments. The space between the Old Testament and the New Testament is called the Intertestamental period. That may sound kind of boring, but what happened in those 400 years was absolutely crazy. It was a time of power, violence, death, and betrayal. As the Old Testament came to a close, the children of Israel were ruled by The Persian Empire. Alexander the Great came along and defeated the Persian king Darius (You have probably heard of him. If you think this is just boring history, check out this 4-minute clip depicting the battle: https://www.youtube.com/watch? v=Uev-cbyAzYc) When Alexander won, he wanted Greek culture to be promoted in every place that he conquered. This was known as Hellenization. As a result, the Hebrew Old Testament was translated into Greek and became known as The Septuagint. (This translation is quoted a lot in the New Testament.) When Alexander died, his territory was divided up among his generals. God's people found themselves under the rule of an evil Seleucid king named Antiochus Epiphananes. Around 167 BC, he overthrew the rightful line of the Jewish priesthood and desecrated the temple. He built a pagan altar to sacrifice unclean animals in it as a godless and defiant act. The Jewish people were furious and a rebellion rose against him. It was led by Judas Maccabaeus, the Braveheart of his period. He was a really skilled warrior, and his leadership established the Hasmonean dynasty. The Hasmoneans put the proper priests back in place and rededicated the temple (which is still celebrated today as Hanukkah.) Around 63 BC, Under Pompey’s leadership, the Romans conquered Israel, and they established the Caesars as the rulers over Judea. They installed Herod the Great as the “King of the Jews.” He collaborated with the Romans to collect taxes and keep peace among the people, and eventually, his sons took over for him. The result was that Greek, Roman, and Jewish cultures were mashed together into a tinder box of frustration, power, and longing, waiting for someone to light the match to bring a revolution of freedom to God's people. Enter John the Baptist. When he came as a prophet announcing that the kingdom of God was at hand, the people were filled with hope. At last, God was coming to rescue his people. At last, God was going to step in. John woke the people up from 400 years of sleep. Knowing these details makes a massive difference. It shows us why John's message was so exciting and the people desperate for a messiah like Jesus. God has placed you here and now because there is something he wants to do through you. He has been working to prepare a longing for hope among your generation. He has been preparing the circumstances for you to be here now. I wonder what he has in store for you?
questions: What is one of your favorite opening scenes of a book or movie? What do you like about it?
Do you tend to find history interesting or boring? Why?
How much of the history of the Intertestamental period did you know before reading this?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share one of their favorite opening scenes of a book or movie. Ask why they like it.

thoughts.verse: But when the right time came, God sent his Son, born of a woman, subject to the law. God sent him to buy freedom for us who were slaves to the law, so that he could adopt us as his very own children.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for working in our world. Thank him for having a purpose and a plan and not abandoning us to chaos and chance.

days.title: Surprise, Surprise
days.scripture: LUKE 1:5-25 When Herod was king of Judea, there was a Jewish priest named Zechariah. He was a member of the priestly order of Abijah, and his wife, Elizabeth, was also from the priestly line of Aaron. Zechariah and Elizabeth were righteous in God’s eyes, careful to obey all of the Lord’s commandments and regulations. They had no children because Elizabeth was unable to conceive, and they were both very old. One day Zechariah was serving God in the Temple, for his order was on duty that week. As was the custom of the priests, he was chosen by lot to enter the sanctuary of the Lord and burn incense. While the incense was being burned, a great crowd stood outside, praying. While Zechariah was in the sanctuary, an angel of the Lord appeared to him, standing to the right of the incense altar. Zechariah was shaken and overwhelmed with fear when he saw him. But the angel said, “Don’t be afraid, Zechariah! God has heard your prayer. Your wife, Elizabeth, will give you a son, and you are to name him John. You will have great joy and gladness, and many will rejoice at his birth, for he will be great in the eyes of the Lord. He must never touch wine or other alcoholic drinks. He will be filled with the Holy Spirit, even before his birth. And he will turn many Israelites to the Lord their God. He will be a man with the spirit and power of Elijah. He will prepare the people for the coming of the Lord. He will turn the hearts of the fathers to their children, and he will cause those who are rebellious to accept the wisdom of the godly.” Zechariah said to the angel, “How can I be sure this will happen? I’m an old man now, and my wife is also well along in years.” Then the angel said, “I am Gabriel! I stand in the very presence of God. It was he who sent me to bring you this good news! But now, since you didn’t believe what I said, you will be silent and unable to speak until the child is born. For my words will certainly be fulfilled at the proper time.” Meanwhile, the people were waiting for Zechariah to come out of the sanctuary, wondering why he was taking so long. When he finally did come out, he couldn’t speak to them. Then they realized from his gestures and his silence that he must have seen a vision in the sanctuary. When Zechariah’s week of service in the Temple was over, he returned home. Soon afterward his wife, Elizabeth, became pregnant and went into seclusion for five months.“How kind the Lord is!” she exclaimed. “He has taken away my disgrace of having no children.”
days.reflection: Have you ever had a surprising encounter with God somewhere? Maybe at a youth camp or retreat? Ever felt God show up out of the blue and touch your heart? Maybe on the last night of camp or something like that? Zechariah did. He was the father of John the Baptist and had lived most of his life thinking that God had forgotten him. He was a priest who served in the temple on a rotation. He was close to the things of God and did his duties faithfully. It says that “he was righteous in God's eyes,” and that God knew the care that he took to obey the Lord. Yet, he had a major point of pain. His wife Elizabeth couldn’t have kids. In that day and age, being barren was considered a sign of judgment. Barrenness meant there was no favor on your life. Yet this had not discouraged Zechariah and Elizabeth to stay loyal to God. One day Zechariah went into the temple to do his duties as a priest and he was shocked to find an angel there. In fact, he was totally freaked out. He was “shaken and overwhelmed with fear.” But the angel gave him good news: “You will have great joy and gladness, and many will rejoice at his birth, for he will be great in the eyes of the Lord.” He is going to have a son, and not just anyone. God is going to use him to do great things in his sight. This is wild stuff. Because he struggles to believe what is happening, the angel makes him unable to speak. His friends know he has had a divine encounter, and he heads back home. Shortly after this strange encounter, his wife gets pregnant, and his sorrow and fear are turned to wonder and joy, but he can't really tell anyone that at the moment. Because he can’t talk. Now you may be asking, why is it important to know these details? What on earth do they have to do with John’s life, or more importantly, yours? But here is what you need to remember: there were events that happened before you were born that give insight and clues as to why you are here. The things your parents were going through, the stuff that was happening in the culture. It all matters because it's what has shaped you and led to you being born on this planet. Do you know the details of your birth? Are there any promises or hints of who you would be, or what your calling would be? You should ask your parents and find out. You need to know how your story starts. Go back and see if there are any clues or hints as to what God has for you. You will never know what surprising details could show up if you don’t ask.
questions: Take a moment right now to ask your dad about what was going on when you were born. Were there any special, interesting, or fun circumstances related to your birth?
Do you believe that people see angels anymore in our modern world? Why or why not?
How do you think you would handle seeing an angel in real life?
Ask your Dad to share any interesting details surrounding his birth. Anything funny, dramatic, or special about when he was born? This matters, because you wouldn’t be here without him.

thoughts.verse: You made all the delicate, inner parts of my body and knit me together in my mother’s womb. Thank you for making me so wonderfully complex! Your workmanship is marvelous—how well I know it. You watched me as I was being formed in utter seclusion, as I was woven together in the dark of the womb. You saw me before I was born. Every day of my life was recorded in your book. Every moment was laid out before a single day had passed.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for the gift of life. Thank him for your family and the events surrounding your birth. Ask him for more clues about your calling.

days.title: Here For This
days.scripture: LUKE 1:17 He will be a man with the spirit and power of Elijah. He will prepare the people for the coming of the Lord. He will turn the hearts of the fathers to their children, and he will cause those who are rebellious to accept the wisdom of the godly.”
days.reflection: When you were younger, what did you want to be when you grew up? Most kids say things like: a doctor, or a sports star, or someone famous. It’s amazing how kids change their minds over the course of time, and how few end up doing what they wanted to when they were kids. Every parent wishes they knew who their kid was going to be when they grow up too. But so much of it is a mystery. You never know how a seemingly random event may shape you, a new conversation inspires you, or a one-off meeting fills your heart. But as a Christian, you can be sure that God will be working to guide you in his path and into his will. Proverbs 16:9 says, “In their hearts humans plan their course, but the LORD establishes their steps.” God had a mission for John the Baptist and it was an important one. God was going to use him to shake his people in a radical way. He was going to make him a prophet to heal father's and son's relationships, create a hunger in people's hearts for the coming of Jesus, and help rebellious people give up their foolish ways to find God's wisdom. If you trust God and walk with him, he will lead you into your purpose too. This is so important as you grow up and become a man. You don’t want to go to a random college, get a random job, marry a random woman, and live a random life. God has more for you than a series of accidents and thoughtless decisions. You want to find out what God has for you. You want to go where he leads you to school, to work where he wants you to, to marry the right woman he has prepared for you, and to live an intentional life. You are not an accident. Your choices really matter. God has put you here because there is important work for you to do. And though your calling may not be as dramatic or famous as John’s, it still matters. He wants to use you to help heal relationships, point people to Jesus so they can be freed from their shame, and give others hope. And you won’t just come in the spirit and power of Elijah but in the spirit and power of Jesus. Who knows what cool stuff power like that can accomplish? Keep searching out what God has for you, and keep moving forward. You never know what can happen on a day like today.
questions: Do you have any idea what you want to be when you grow up? Any areas that interest you? How did you initially get interested in those areas?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share if there are any promises, bible passages, or things God has put in his heart to pray for you. How did he choose these things to pray for you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share one or two ways they figured out God's will for their life. How did they sense God leading and guiding them?

thoughts.verse: The LORD says, “I will guide you along the best pathway for your life. I will advise you and watch over you. Do not be like a senseless horse or mule that needs a bit and bridle to keep it under control.”
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and lift up any areas of your life where you feel like you need wisdom from God or his direction. Ask him to make his will clear to you so that you can follow his path.

days.title: Say My Name
days.scripture: LUKE 1:57-79 When it was time for Elizabeth’s baby to be born, she gave birth to a son. And when her neighbors and relatives heard that the Lord had been very merciful to her, everyone rejoiced with her. When the baby was eight days old, they all came for the circumcision ceremony. They wanted to name him Zechariah, after his father. But Elizabeth said, “No! His name is John!” “What?” they exclaimed. “There is no one in all your family by that name.” So they used gestures to ask the baby’s father what he wanted to name him. He motioned for a writing tablet, and to everyone’s surprise he wrote, “His name is John.” Instantly Zechariah could speak again, and he began praising God. Awe fell upon the whole neighborhood, and the news of what had happened spread throughout the Judean hills. Everyone who heard about it reflected on these events and asked, “What will this child turn out to be?” For the hand of the Lord was surely upon him in a special way. Then his father, Zechariah, was filled with the Holy Spirit and gave this prophecy: “Praise the Lord, the God of Israel, because he has visited and redeemed his people. He has sent us a mighty Savior from the royal line of his servant David, just as he promised through his holy prophets long ago. Now we will be saved from our enemies and from all who hate us. He has been merciful to our ancestors by remembering his sacred covenant— the covenant he swore with an oath to our ancestor Abraham. We have been rescued from our enemies so we can serve God without fear, in holiness and righteousness for as long as we live. “And you, my little son, will be called the prophet of the Most High, because you will prepare the way for the Lord. You will tell his people how to find salvation through forgiveness of their sins. Because of God’s tender mercy, the morning light from heaven is about to break upon us, to give light to those who sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, and to guide us to the path of peace.”
days.reflection: What is your name? Literally, what is your full name? Take a moment to think about it. Do you know what it means? Do you know why your parents called you that? What did they hope to communicate to you and to others by calling you that? Names are important. A name is how you are known. It’s how you are remembered. It reveals your parents’ intentions for you in some remarkable ways. When John the Baptist was born, people assumed he would be named after his Father. That was part of the custom of the day. But God wanted to name John because he wanted it to reflect his calling. John means, “graced by God” or “God is gracious.” John was going to play a role in God's story of redemption and grace for his people, and not just a part of Zechariah and Elizabeth’s story. His name was to reflect his calling. Naming things can be powerful. You can call people names and beat them down. You can make fun of their physical appearance with a name or make fun of mistakes they have made. But you can also encourage people by how you name them. Being given a cool nickname by people who like you can be special. You also have a surname, and that is really important too. It speaks of your family’s history and connects you to the reputation of the men who have gone before you. And believe it or not, at some point you will probably have kids of your own and name them. And when you do, you will be trying to communicate something to the world. Your family has a history and story that you are called to extend. You need to carry your family’s legacy into the next generation. But more importantly, God has a legacy for you in his kingdom, You need to live up to what he has called you to do. Proverbs 22:1 says, “Choose a good reputation over great riches; being held in high esteem is better than silver or gold.” A man’s name matters; work in making yours what you are destined to be.
questions: Do you have a nickname? What is it and how did you get it? How do you feel about it?
Ask your dad why they named you what they did? What did they hope to convey to people by giving you your name?
Ask your dad why his parents chose his name? In what way has his name impacted his life?

thoughts.verse: But now, O Jacob, listen to the LORD who created you. O Israel, the one who formed you says, “Do not be afraid, for I have ransomed you. I have called you by name; you are mine. When you go through deep waters, I will be with you. When you go through rivers of difficulty, you will not drown. When you walk through the fire of oppression, you will not be burned up; the flames will not consume you.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Thank God for your name. Ask him to help you live up to your calling and purpose and build a good reputation for his glory.

extras: WEEK 1 MANSCHOOL FAMILY TREE MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: FAMILY TREE If your son is going to live well, he has to know about your family’s history and story. Spend an evening drawing out and explaining your family tree to him. What interesting events and people are a part of your family history? What sins and stains have impacted your family? Try and make this creative. See if you can find old photos or video footage to help bring this to light. See how many generations you can go back, and what implications earlier generations’ decisions have made now. OPTION 2. WATCH STAR-WARS EPISODE ONE You have probably seen this already, but this time ask, in what ways does the context and backstory revealed in this film help you understand the previous films? In what way has learning about the intertestamental period helped your understanding of New Testament context? Alternative Option: Watch a movie neither of you have seen. Skip the first ten minutes. See about how long it takes you to figure out the plot of the film by missing the beginning’s framing context. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Recap the week and spend some time sharing as much as you can about your family history. Give him a sense of context for his story, and some of the key points leading up to his birth. Tell him about what it means to be in your family and to carry the family name forward. Talk about some things to celebrate and cautions to avoid from previous generations.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
